content.problemStatement:
We have a square matrix of size N×NN \times NN×N, each cell is colored either white or black. You have to go from (1,1)(1 , 1)(1,1) to (N,N)(N , N)(N,N) by traveling on a cell with only one color, for the same you can change the color of some cells. From a cell you can go in all four directions (up, left, down and right). More formally, from cell (i,j)(i , j)(i,j) you can go to cell (i−1,j)(i-1 , j)(i−1,j), (i,j−1)(i , j-1)(i,j−1), (i+1,j)(i+1 , j)(i+1,j) and (i,j+1)(i , j+1)(i,j+1).

You have to find out a minimum number of changes required so that you can reach (N,N)(N , N)(N,N) from (1,1)(1 , 1)(1,1).


Input Format

The first line of input contains 222 integers NNN - number of columns and rows in the matrix.
Next NNN lines each contain NNN space separated integers where jthj^{th}jth integer in ithi^{th}ith line denotes color of cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), 111 means black and 000 means white.


Output Format
Output a single integer - minimum number of color changes required.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Case 1 : Choose white (0) as path color then one of the best paths would be (1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)(1,1) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (2,2)(1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)
Number of changes = 2 for changing color of cell (1,2)(1,2)(1,2) and (2,2)(2,2)(2,2).
Case 2 : Choose black (1) as path color then one of the best paths would be  (1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)(1,1) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (2,2)(1,1)→(1,2)→(2,2)
Number of changes = 1 for changing color of cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1).

Among both the cases minimum number of changes = 1
